svg.text: TOK PISIN
HISTORY & CREOLIZATION
PAPUA NEW GUINEA LINGUISTIC LANDSCAPE
PNG: ~840 languages — most linguistically diverse country on Earth
8M people • 840 languages • = one language per ~9,500 people
Tok Pisin arose as lingua franca to bridge this diversity
ORIGIN OF TOK PISIN
19th c.: South Pacific Pidgin English on trade and plantation ships
Sandalwood trade, labor recruiting, missionary contact — English spread
1880s-1920s: German colonial period in PNG; Tok Pisin stabilizes
1975: PNG independence; Tok Pisin declared official language
CREOLIZATION: PIDGIN BECOMES LANGUAGE
Pidgin: simplified contact language with no native speakers
Creole: pidgin acquired as first language by children
Creolization adds: pronouns, grammar rules, expanded vocabulary
Tok Pisin: completed creolization in 20th century; 2-4M native speakers
ENGLISH HISTORY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
ENGLISH IN PAPUA NEW GUINEA
1884: British colonize SE Papua; English officially introduced
Australian administration (1906-1975): English language schooling
English: official language of PNG alongside Tok Pisin + Hiri Motu
But: only ~2% of PNG population speaks English as L1
WHY ENGLISH EXPANDED GLOBALLY
British Empire: covered 25% of Earth's land area at peak (1920)
Industrial Revolution, trade, science, technology in English
USA dominance post-WWII: Hollywood, internet, academia
Today: ~1.5B English speakers worldwide (L1 + L2)
THE CREOLE PARADOX
Tok Pisin: child of English, but grammatically independent
English speakers cannot understand Tok Pisin without study
"Gras bilong fes i no save gro long ston."
"Grass of face does not know how to grow on stone."
= "A beard cannot grow on a stone." (proverb)
New language; same root; distant enough to be a separate tongue
PLATE 45d ALT · TOK PISIN / ENGLISH · HISTORY
